volumeTitle: Stichus. Trinummus. Truculentus. Tale of a Travelling Bag. Fragments
label: Truculentus (Section 2.7.615 - Section 4.2.711)

sections.label: Section 2.7.615
sections.original: si tu ín legione béllator clues , át ego in culina clúeo .
sections.translation: If you have the renown of a warrior with your troops , still I ’ m a Mars [*] in the kitchen .

sections.label: Section 2.7.616
sections.original: Si aequóm facias , advéntores meos nón incuses , quórum
sections.translation: If you did the thing that ’ s right , you ’ d not be abusing my visitors , whose gifts I hold as acceptable and pleasing , and your own which I have received of you as unacceptable .

sections.label: Section 2.7.618
sections.original: Tum pól ego et donis prívatus sum et périi .
sections.translation: Then , i ’ faith , I ’ m both deprived of my presents and undone as well !

sections.label: Section 2.7.618b
sections.original: Plane istúc est .
sections.translation: Clearly it is so .

sections.label: Section 2.7.619
sections.original: Quíd nunc ergo hic ódiosu'sodiosus es , confessus omnino reus ?
sections.translation: Why then are you now here , with your annoyance , who confess that you are worsted in every point ?

sections.label: Section 2.7.620
sections.original: Périi hercle hodie , nísi hunc a te abigo .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , I ’ m this day undone , if I don ’ t drive this fellow away from you .

sections.label: Section 2.7.620b
sections.original: Áccede huc modo , adi húc modo .
sections.translation: Only approach this way ; only step this way !

sections.label: Section 2.7.621
sections.original: Étiam , scelus virí , minitare ? quém ego offatim iam iam iam cóncipilabo .
sections.translation: Scoundrelly fellow , threatening even ? Whom this very , very , very instant I ’ ll be chopping up into splinters . What business have you coming here ? What business have you to approach her ? ( Pointing to PHRONESIUM .) What business have you , I say , to be knowing my mistress ? You shall die this instant if you make the slightest movement with your hand .

sections.label: Section 2.7.625
sections.original: Quid , manu vicerim ?
sections.translation: Why shouldn ’ t I move my hand ?

sections.label: Section 2.7.625b
sections.original: Fac quod iussi , mane .
sections.translation: Do as I commanded ; stop ; I ’ ll this instant cut you up into mincemeat on the spot .

sections.label: Section 2.7.627
sections.original: Cáptiost : istám machaeram lóngiorem habes quam haéc est .
sections.translation: It ’ s a ticklish point ; you have a longer sword there than this is ( pointing to a knife in his girdle ) ; but just let me go seek a spit , if indeed I must be having a battle with you . I ’ ll be off home . Warrior , for me and you I ’ ll choose an impartial judge .

sections.label: Section 2.7.630
sections.original: sed ego cesso hinc me amoliri , ventre dum salvo licet ?—
sections.translation: ( Aside .) But why am I delaying to betake myself off hence , while with a safe inside I may ?

sections.label: Section 2.8.631
sections.original: Datin sóleas ? atque me intro actutum ducite ,
sections.translation: Give me my sandals [*] , and take me at once in-doors ; for my head aches shockingly from the air .

sections.label: Section 2.8.633
sections.original: Quid mihi futurum est , cui duae ancillae dolent ,
sections.translation: What ’ s to become of me , to whom the two female slaves cause ache enough , with which I presented you ? ( PHRONESIUM is led into the house .) Are you off then ? Well thus one ’ s used in return . How can you possibly shut me out . ( The door is slammed to .)

sections.label: Section 2.8.635
sections.original: quo pacto excludi , quaeso , potui planius ,
sections.translation: Prithee , can anything be more clear than that I ’ m now shut out ? I ’ m finely fooled . Be it so . With how little difficulty ( placing his foot against the door ) might I now be persuaded to break the ankles of this entire mansion ! Do the manners of covetous women change at all ?

sections.label: Section 2.8.640
sections.original: postquam filiolum peperit , animos sustulit .
sections.translation: Since she has brought forth a son , she has plucked her spirit up . Now it ’ s as though she said to me , I neither ask you nor forbid you to come into the house . But I won ’ t — I shan ’ t go — I ’ ll make her to be saying in a very few days that I ’ m a cruel man . ( To his ATTENDANTS .) Follow me this way . A word ’ s enough .

sections.label: Section 3.1.645
sections.original: Rus mane dudum hinc ire me iussit pater ,
sections.translation: One morning a short time since my father ordered me to go hence , to deal out the mast for food for the oxen . After I got there a person arrived at the farmhouse ( so it pleased the Gods ), who was owing money to my father , who had formerly purchased some Tarentine sheep of my father ;

sections.label: Section 3.1.650
sections.original: quaerit patrem . dico esse in urbe . interrogo ,
sections.translation: he asked for my father ; I said he was in the city ; I enquired what he wanted with him . The fellow takes a purse from off his neck , and gives me twenty minae ; with pleasure I receive them ,

sections.label: Section 3.1.654
sections.original: condo in cruminam . ille abit . ego propere minas
sections.translation: and stow them in my purse ; these bad sheep [*] , the minae , have I brought in my purse hither to the city .

sections.label: Section 3.1.656
sections.original: fuít edepol Mars meo periratus patri ,
sections.translation: By my troth , Mars has proved very angry with my father ; for his sheep are not very far away from the wolves [*] . Now , with this one stroke shall I send adrift those finical town gallants , and be bundling them all out of doors .

sections.label: Section 3.1.660
sections.original: eradicarest certum cumprimis patrem ,
sections.translation: My father , in the first place , I ’ m quite resolved to ruin , root and branch ; then next in turn , my mother . Now to-day I ’ ll carry this money to her whom I love more than my own mother . ( Goes towards the door of PHRONESIUM , and knocks .) Hillo there — is any one here ? There ’ s not a woman . Is any one going to open this door ?

sections.label: Section 3.1.664_665
sections.original: Quid istúc ? alienun es , amabo , mi Strabax ,
sections.translation: Why so a stranger , pray , my dear Strabax ?

sections.label: Section 3.1.666
sections.original: qui non extemplo intro ieris ?
sections.translation: Why don ’ t you come in at once ?

sections.label: Section 3.1.666b
sections.original: Anne oportuit ?
sections.translation: Ought you to have been doing so ,

sections.label: Section 3.1.667
sections.original: Ita te quidem , qui es familiaris .
sections.translation: you , indeed , who are so intimate ?

sections.label: Section 3.1.667b
sections.original: Ibitur ,
sections.translation: I ’ ll go in then , that you mayn ’ t think I ’ m loitering .

sections.label: Section 3.1.668b
sections.original: Lepide facis .
sections.translation: You act obligingly .

sections.label: Section 3.2.669
sections.original: Mirum videtur , rure erilem filium
sections.translation: It seems marvellous to me , that Strabax , my master ’ s son ,

sections.label: Section 3.2.670
sections.original: Strabacem non rediisse ; nisi si clanculum
sections.translation: hasn ’ t returned from the country , unless perchance he has slily slipt in here into this den of corruption of his .

sections.label: Section 3.2.672
sections.original: Iam pol illic inclamabit me si aspexerit .
sections.translation: Now , faith , he ’ ll be roaring at me if he espies me .

sections.label: Section 3.2.673
sections.original: Nimio minus saévos iam sum , Astaphium , quam fui ,
sections.translation: I ’ m much less savage now , Astaphium , than I was before ; I ’ m not churlish now ; don ’ t thee fear . ( She runs to a distance .) What wouldst thee be at ? What ?

sections.label: Section 3.2.675
sections.original: Quid vis ? qui d metuam ? tuam expecto truculentiam .
sections.translation: What , say you ? Why , I ’ m waiting for your churlishness .

sections.label: Section 3.2.676
sections.original: Dic impera mihi quid libet quo vis modo .
sections.translation: Say , command me what thee dost please , and in what way thee dost please . I ’ ve got all my manners anew : my old ones I ’ ve parted with . I can e ’ en fall in love , or take a mistress now .

sections.label: Section 3.2.679
sections.original: Lepide mecastor nuntias . sed dic mihi ,
sections.translation: Upon my faith , you do tell me fine news . But tell me ,

sections.label: Section 3.2.680
sections.original: haben —
sections.translation: have you — ?

sections.label: Section 3.2.680b
sections.original: Marsipum te fortasse dicere ?
sections.translation: A mistress [*] , perhaps , thee means .

sections.label: Section 3.2.681
sections.original: Intellexisti lepide quid ego dicerem .
sections.translation: You ’ ve understood nicely what I meant to say .

sections.label: Section 3.2.682
sections.original: Heus tu , iam postquam in urbem crebro commeo ,
sections.translation: Hark you , since I ’ ve been so many times backwards and forwards to the city , I ’ ve become quite a chatterer ; I ’ m now a right good stalker [*] .

sections.label: Section 3.2.684
sections.original: Quid id est , amabo ? ístaec ridicularia ,
sections.translation: Prithee , what ’ s that ? That ’ s nonsense ;

sections.label: Section 3.2.685
sections.original: cavillátiones , vis opinor dicere ?
sections.translation: perhaps you intend to mean talking .

sections.label: Section 3.2.686
sections.original: Istud pauxillum differt a cavillibus .
sections.translation: Just so ; it differs mighty little from stalking .

sections.label: Section 3.2.687
sections.original: Sequere intro me , amabo , mea voluptas .
sections.translation: Prithee , do follow me in-doors , my love .

sections.label: Section 3.2.687b
sections.original: Tene hoc tibi :
sections.translation: Take this for thyself ; keep it as a ledger [*] for thee , that thee mayst give me thy company this night .

sections.label: Section 3.2.689
sections.original: Perii , rabonem ? quam esse dicam hanc beluam ?
sections.translation: You are the death of me , with your ledger . What kind of beast am I to say that is ?

sections.label: Section 3.2.690
sections.original: quin tu arrabonem dicis ?
sections.translation: Thy don ’ t you say pledge ?

sections.label: Section 3.2.690b
sections.original: A facio lucri ,
sections.translation: The r I make a saving of ; just as the Praenestines [*] have conia , for ciconia .

sections.label: Section 3.2.692
sections.original: Sequere , obsecro .
sections.translation: Prithee , do follow me .

sections.label: Section 3.2.692b
sections.original: Strabacem hic opperiar modo ,
sections.translation: I ’ ll wait here a little for Strabax , till he comes from the farm .

sections.label: Section 3.2.693b
sections.original: Is quidem hic ápud nos est Strabax ,
sections.translation: Why , Strabax is at our house . He has just come from the farm .

sections.label: Section 3.2.694b
sections.original: Priusne quam ad matrem suam ?
sections.translation: What , before he went to his own mother ?

sections.label: Section 3.2.695
sections.original: eu edepol hominem nihili .
sections.translation: Alas , the man ’ s worth nought , i ’ faith .

sections.label: Section 3.2.695b
sections.original: Iamne autem ut soles ?
sections.translation: What now , your old habit ?

sections.label: Section 3.2.696
sections.original: Iamne nihil dico ?
sections.translation: Well , I ’ ll say nought .

sections.label: Section 3.2.696b
sections.original: I intro , amabo , cedo manum .
sections.translation: Prithee , do come indoors . Give me your hand .

sections.label: Section 3.2.697
sections.original: Tene . in tabernam ducor devorsoriam ,
sections.translation: Well , take it . ( To the AUDIENCE .) I ’ m being led off into a public-house , where I shall be but poorly entertained for my money .

sections.label: Section 4.1.699
sections.original: Néque gnatust neque progignetur neque potest reperirier
sections.translation: There ’ s not a person born , nor will there be born , nor can there be found one ,

sections.label: Section 4.1.700
sections.original: cuí ego nunc aut dictum aut factum melius quam Veneri velim .
sections.translation: to whom I would now wish praises to be given , or on whom attentions bestowed , rather than on Venus . Ye great Gods , how joyous I am , and how I ’ m transported with joyousness ! Such great tidings of joy has Cyamus brought to me this day ; that my presents have been esteemed and deemed acceptable by Phronesium . While this now is a delight , then besides this in especial is rare honey-drink to me ,

sections.label: Section 4.1.705
sections.original: militis odiosa ingrataque habita . totus gaudeo .
sections.translation: that the Captain ’ s presents are held as disagreable and not acceptable . I ’ m all enraptured ! The ball ’ s my own [*] ; if the Captain ’ s sent adrift , the woman will be mine . I ’ m saved , because I ’ m going to ruin ; if I didn ’ t go to ruin , it ’ s clear I should die . Now I ’ ll keep watch , what ’ s going on there , who goes into the house , who comes out of doors ; from here at a distance will I observe what is to be my lot .

sections.label: Section 4.1.710
sections.original: quia nihil habeo unum animos movi mi hic omnia agam precario †
sections.translation: Because I ’ ve got nothing , my feelings remind me of one thing ; I ’ ll do everything by begging .

sections.label: Section 4.2.711
sections.original: Lepide éfficiam meum ego ófficium : vide intús modo ut tu tuom item éfficias .
sections.translation: I ’ ll cleverly do my duty , mistress ; do you only take care that in-doors you do yours as well ; love that which you ought , your own interest ; clean that fellow thoroughly out . Now , while it pleases the fellow , while he has got something , adapt the opportunity to that purpose . Display all your charms to your lover that you may heighten his joys .
